entities.description: The anonymous fifteenth-century Middle English prose treatise on angling: its equipment, lines, hooks, floats, methods, conditions, baits, fish, and ethical use of the sport. A traditional attribution to Juliana Berners is disputed and is not treated as established authorship.
entities.label: Treatyse of Fysshynge wyth an Angle
notes: This abstract work entity separates intellectual content from the incomplete manuscript, 1496 printed version, 1883 composite edition, and modern representations.

rights.basis: The fifteenth-century work and witnesses are centuries outside copyright term. Modern editions, markup, photographs, and provider terms remain separately scoped.
rights.component: Medieval Middle English and embedded Latin linguistic and literary content

bibliographic.citation: An Older Form of the Treatyse of Fysshynge wyth an Angle, attributed to Dame Juliana Barnes, printed from a MS. in the possession of Alfred Denison, Esq., with preface and glossary by Thomas Satchell (London: W. Satchell & Co., 1883).
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Publications no. 41 / no. 9 in volume XIX (society not responsible for the publication)
bibliographic.title: An Older Form of the Treatyse of Fysshynge wyth an Angle

entities.description: Satchell's edition prints the surviving medieval manuscript text and supplies missing matter from the 1496 printed Treatyse in square brackets. Its preface describes the manuscript, transmission, editorial method, and W. W. Skeat's assistance; the volume also includes a glossary and advertisements not encoded in CME00132.

notes: Printed by S. Peyton, 11 Bartholomew Close, E.C.

dateStatements.label: Medieval manuscript production
dateStatements.notes: The current Yale catalog retains a broad fifteenth-century date; Skeat's pre-1450 view is historical opinion, not normalized as the current catalog date.

entities.description: A fifteenth-century English paper manuscript preserving twenty pages of the treatise in one bold English secretary hand. Three leaves are missing between manuscript pages 10 and 11 and seven leaves at the end. In its present nineteenth-century format, each original page labeled 1b–20b is interleaved with Haslewood's paginary transcript labeled 1a–20a, together with added illuminations and prefatory matter.

holding.notes: Purchased through A. S. W. Rosenbach after the Denison sale, later owned by David Wagstaff, and given to Yale in 1946 by Mrs. David Wagstaff.

entities.label: Yale, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, MS 171
notes: Provenance recorded by Yale: William Herbert; Rev. John Brand; George Isted; Joseph Haslewood; Edward Jesse; Alfred Denison; A. S. W. Rosenbach; David Wagstaff; Yale.
Satchell's preface reports the 1868 Jesse-sale purchase by Denison for 45 shillings and describes five paper sheets folded quarto.

Satchell says four earlier 'pages' are wanting, while Yale says three 'leaves' are missing between manuscript pages 10 and 11. The difference in units and physical count is preserved rather than silently reconciled.

physicalDescription.extent: 10 medieval paper leaves (20 original pages), interleaved with 10 modern transcript leaves (20 paginated transcript pages), plus 2 paper leaves
physicalDescription.format: Codex fragment; no ruling or pricking; one bold English secretary hand; original leaves glued to modern stubs
notes: Simple flourishes and initial strokes are red; leaves are stained and severely trimmed with loss of marginalia.
Three medieval leaves are missing between pages 10 and 11 and seven leaves at the end.
The nineteenth-century brick-red calf binding was gold-tooled by C. Lewis in 1823 and later rebacked.

rights.basis: The medieval text and manuscript are centuries outside copyright term. Physical ownership and rights in modern digital images remain separate.
rights.component: Fifteenth-century manuscript textual content

entities.description: A nineteenth-century handwritten transcript of the twenty surviving medieval pages, created for convenience and interleaved with the originals in the current bound object. Digital labels 1a–20a identify the transcript pages, while 1b–20b identify the medieval originals. The digitized order alternates by openings: it begins 1a, 1b, 2b, 2a, 3a, 3b, 4b, 4a and continues in that pattern.

holding.notes: Material component of the same present bound object as the medieval original.

entities.label: Joseph Haslewood's paginary transcript interleaved in Beinecke MS 171
notes: Satchell calls this a transcript made for convenience but states that his edition was printed from the manuscript; no relation routes Satchell's transcription through Haslewood.

physicalDescription.format: Paginary manuscript transcript interleaved with the medieval leaves
notes: Haslewood also added three illuminations and prefatory matter before arranging the current format and 1823 binding.

rights.basis: The transcript was created by 1823 and Haslewood died in 1833; it is outside United States copyright term. Yale's modern images are separately scoped.
rights.component: Joseph Haslewood's nineteenth-century transcript and arrangement

bibliographic.citation: Book of hawking, hunting, and heraldry (Westminster: Wynkyn de Worde, 1496), containing 'Here begynneth the treatyse of fysshynge wyth an angle' on leaves [37b]–[48b].
bibliographic.title: Book of hawking, hunting, and heraldry

entities.description: The Westminster 1496 printed edition commonly cataloged as the Book of Hawking, Hunting, and Heraldry. It adds the angling treatise on leaves [37b]–[48b]. Satchell says he supplied gaps from the printed Treatyse, but his precise physical copy or intervening facsimile is not identified.
entities.label: Wynkyn de Worde's 1496 Boke of Hawking, Hunting, and Heraldry, including the Treatyse of Fysshynge
notes: The entity type reflects the lineage schema's printed-edition category; this is a historical print edition, not a modern critical edition.
The 1486 Boke did not yet contain the fishing treatise; the angling component first appears in this enlarged 1496 print.

rights.basis: The 1496 printed edition is centuries outside copyright term. Rights and provider terms for modern reproductions are separate.
rights.component: 1496 printed textual content, arrangement, and typography

entities.description: The Library of Congress copy of the 1496 Wynkyn de Worde edition, call number Incun. 1496 .B4 SK25. Its 23-surface Treatyse run is complete at digital images 85–107; the copy's cataloged facsimile substitutions at leaves [25], [67], and [74] fall outside that run.

holding.notes: Rare Book and Special Collections Division.

None of the cataloged facsimile substitutions falls within the Treatyse on leaves [37b]–[48b].

next_steps: Consult Julia Boffey's full ODNB discussion and the specialized studies cited by the Yale catalog, especially John McDonald and W. L. Braekman.
Keep the 1486 hunting explicit, the anonymous 1496 fishing text, and later compilation-level catalog attributions separate.
Retain anonymous authorship with a disputed traditional attribution unless document-level evidence identifies the angling author.
notes: Provider creator headings and Satchell's title-page phrase 'attributed to' are evidence of attribution history, not proof of authorship.
openQuestions.question: Can any evidence securely attribute the angling treatise itself to Juliana Berners, rather than extending the hunting section's named explicit to the later 1496 fishing addition?

next_steps: Inspect Satchell's correspondence in the Beinecke files and contemporary publication records for an identified print or facsimile.
Compare Satchell's supplied readings against multiple 1496 copies and nineteenth-century facsimiles for copy-specific or intermediary variants.
Do not identify Library of Congress Rosenwald 574 as Satchell's exemplar without provenance or textual evidence.
openQuestions.question: Which physical copy, facsimile, or intermediary of the 1496 printed Treatyse did Satchell use for his two supplied passages?

next_steps: Ask Beinecke staff for the quire/stub evidence supporting the current three-leaf loss statement.
Inspect Satchell's correspondence and any older collations to determine what he counted as a 'page.'
Do not convert pages to leaves or choose a single internal-loss count without a physical-structure crosswalk.
notes: Satchell's statement is at printed preface p. iv / cached PDF p. 14; Yale locates its three missing leaves between manuscript pages 10 and 11.
openQuestions.question: How should Satchell's statement that four earlier manuscript pages were wanting be reconciled with Yale's current statement that three leaves are missing between manuscript pages 10 and 11?

next_steps: Consult modern linguistic and textual studies rather than normalizing Skeat's pre-1450 opinion as a settled work date.
Compare manuscript and 1496 versions to distinguish textual formation from physical witness production.
Keep England as cultural origin while leaving narrower composition place unknown unless independent evidence supports it.
openQuestions.question: When and where was the angling treatise composed, as distinct from the date and dialect of Beinecke MS 171 and the 1496 Westminster printing?

next_steps: Check Yale and Beinecke reproduction policies at the point of intended use.
Ask Yale for a work-specific rights determination if images are to be redistributed commercially or in print-on-demand output.
Do not infer an open license solely from public IIIF and PDF access.
notes: The IIIF manifest's rights field is absent/null; its requiredStatement only names Yale University Library as provider.
openQuestions.question: What current reuse terms govern Yale's MS 171 digital photographs and generated PDF?

reviewNotes: The intellectual work cataloged in the parallel metadata manifest is the anonymous Middle English angling treatise, not Satchell's editorial paratext, a physical witness, the 1496 carrier volume, the digital encodings, or the facsimiles.

CME00132 includes the 1883 title page (PB REF 1) and treatise pp. 1–37 (PB REF 2–38, followed by terminal REF 39); it excludes Satchell's presentation leaf, preface i–vii, ten-page glossary, and advertisements.
The XML title page reads 'Alfred Dension'; the inspected 1883 title page and manuscript provenance establish the owner's surname as Alfred Denison.

Satchell's preface, printed preface p. iv / cached PDF p. 14, says that 'four of the earlier pages are also wanting,' whereas Yale's current catalog says three leaves are missing between manuscript pages 10 and 11. Page and leaf units are not normalized into a single physical-loss count; the discrepancy remains open pending a codicological explanation.

Satchell's first printed supply begins on p. 9 after 'the grener hyt wyl be' and ends on p. 15 before 'Wyth how many herys ye schall angle ...'; his second begins on p. 23 after the October trout-bait sentence and continues through the p. 37 explicit. The second supply has no visible terminal square bracket, so its endpoint is recorded by phrase and explicit, not by an invented mark.

The Beinecke PDF has 75 PDF pages because it adds a generated opening page to a 74-canvas IIIF sequence. The medieval original occupies the twenty canvases labeled 1b–20b; the interleaved 1a–20a pages are Haslewood's later transcript.

The Library of Congress target run is visually confirmed at PDF/images 85–107 inclusive: image 85 begins the Treatyse and image 107 contains its explicit before the next work. This corresponds to carrier leaves [37b]–[48b].

Within that full 1496 run, Satchell's first printed supply maps exactly to LOC images 90–93 / source IDs 0090–0093, from 'A nother wyse ye maye make more bryghter grene' mid-image 90 through 'now I woll tell you' near the foot of image 93. His second maps to images 99–107 / IDs 0099–0107, from 'From Aprill till Septembre the trought lepyth' mid-image 99 through the explicit on image 107.

The cached Library of Congress PDF is complete and readable as 167 pages, but qpdf reports a damaged/cross-reference reconstruction, extraneous whitespace before xref, and linearization hint-table/page-length mismatches. It is therefore not described as qpdf-clean.

Juliana Berners is a traditional and disputed attribution, not a confirmed author. The named 'Dam Julyans Barnes' explicit belongs to the hunting section of the earlier Boke tradition, and later cataloging extended that attribution to the angling treatise.
Private Git-ignored caches preserve complete provider objects; caching does not itself authorize redistribution.

lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is a repository copy of University of Michigan item CME00132 and encodes the title page plus the complete treatise on printed pp. 1–37 of Thomas Satchell's 1883 An Older Form of the Treatyse of Fysshynge wyth an Angle. It excludes Satchell's presentation leaf, preface, glossary, and advertisements. Satchell printed the surviving portions of the fifteenth-century paper manuscript now Yale, Beinecke MS 171, while marking two extensive supplies from the 1496 Wynkyn de Worde printed Treatyse: from p. 9 into p. 15 and from p. 23 through the p. 37 explicit. The current Beinecke binding also interleaves Joseph Haslewood's nineteenth-century paginary transcript (pages labeled 1a–20a) with the medieval original (1b–20b); Satchell describes the transcript as a convenience but says his edition was printed from the manuscript, so the edition is related directly to the medieval pages rather than routed through Haslewood. Complete public digital objects are cached for the 1883 edition, Beinecke MS 171, and Library of Congress Rosenwald 574, whose leaves [37b]–[48b] preserve the 1496 printed treatise used here as edition-level evidence rather than as Satchell's demonstrable physical exemplar.

catalogingSubject.label: Treatyse of Fysshynge wyth an Angle
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the anonymous fifteenth-century Middle English prose treatise on angling represented by the complete text on pp. 1–37 of Thomas Satchell's 1883 composite edition. Satchell combined two surviving runs of Beinecke MS 171 with two passages supplied from the 1496 Wynkyn de Worde printed Treatyse. The work includes its proem comparing sports, practical instruction on tackle, methods, conditions, fish and baits, and the final ethical order, but excludes the 1883 source-edition title page, presentation leaf, preface, glossary, advertisements, and all digital-representation layers. Juliana Berners is retained only as a traditional disputed attribution, not as a confirmed author.

extent.description: One prose treatise on printed pp. 1–37, encoded as 17 principal DIV2 sections plus six nested month DIV3 sections. Two short Latin verse quotations comprise four L elements; they are embedded maxims and do not change the prose work-level form. The separate source-edition title-page DIV1 is excluded from work extent.

assertion.notes: Brief embedded verse quotations are recorded in language and extent facets but do not warrant a work-level mixed-form label.

formStatements.description: A continuous prose treatise organized by practical headings. Four Latin lines occur as two brief quoted maxims and are immediately explained in prose.

assertion.notes: The text gives procedural instruction for rods, lines, hooks, floats, fishing conditions, fish, and baits.
The work presents angling as healthful, morally regulated recreation and contrasts it with hunting, hawking, and fowling.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Modern English on the 1883 title page and in Satchell's excluded preface/glossary is an edition layer, not the primary language of the medieval work.

assertion.notes: The embedded Latin quotations do not make the prose treatise intrinsically mixed-form.

scope.description: Two short paired maxims, four verse lines total, each followed by Middle English explanation

notes: The XML source title-page typo 'Alfred Dension' is corrected to Alfred Denison only in explanatory metadata; the encoded artifact remains unchanged.
Four Latin verse lines are embedded quotations inside a prose treatise. They are recorded as quoted Latin and in extent metrics, but do not trigger a mixed-form classification.

Satchell's source segmentation is modeled with exact phrase boundaries. The second printed supply has no visible terminal closing square bracket and therefore ends at the explicit rather than an invented punctuation mark.
Satchell's 'four ... earlier pages' and Yale's 'three leaves' are kept as distinct, unresolved physical-loss statements rather than normalized to one number.

Haslewood's interleaved transcript is a distinct supporting component and is not treated as Satchell's direct source because Satchell says the edition was printed from the manuscript and describes the transcript only as a convenience.
The Library of Congress copy is a complete representative witness to the 1496 edition, not Satchell's demonstrated physical exemplar.
No source-edition, witness, digital-facsimile, or provider creator heading is promoted silently into medieval authorship.

assertion.notes: The work is Middle English, its surviving manuscript is cataloged as English, and its first print is Westminster. A narrower composition place is not established.
No inspected document names the angling treatise's author. Anonymous cataloging avoids converting a later compilation-level attribution into fact.

The 1486 named explicit belongs to the hunting section. The fishing treatise was added in 1496, and later cataloging extended the attribution; current scholarship does not establish Berners's authorship of this text.

notes: Traditional catalog headings and title-page wording are retained as attribution history only.
Source-edition responsibility, not medieval intellectual authorship.

scope.description: The 1883 source edition represented by CME00132

assertion.notes: Satchell prints the surviving manuscript portions and explicitly supplies two missing spans from the printed Treatyse.

scope.description: The continuous 1883 edited treatise assembled from manuscript and print

assertion.notes: The bait instructions include six named month subheadings from March through August.
The final order condemns trespass and theft, discourages taking too many fish, and urges nourishment of the game and destruction of its predators.

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English prose treatise presenting angling as a healthful and morally regulated recreation. After comparing fishing favorably with hunting, hawking, and fowling, it teaches the making and coloring of rods and lines, hooks, floats, methods, suitable places, times and weather, impediments, fish-specific and seasonal baits, and the care of bait. Its final order prohibits trespass, theft, and excessive taking, urges stewardship of fish stocks, and frames solitary angling as conducive to bodily health, prayer, and avoidance of idleness. Two brief Latin maxims—four lines total—are quoted and explained in Middle English. The CME text represents Satchell's 1883 composite edition, which joins two surviving manuscript runs to two passages supplied from the 1496 printed Treatyse.

The encoded treatise has a prose proem with two paired Latin maxims; comparisons of hawking, fowling, and fishing; practical divisions on making rods, coloring lines, hair strength, floats, kinds of angling, place, time, weather, impediments, salmon and trout baits, and six month subsections from March to August; and a terminal ethical order. The XML organizes these as 17 DIV2 sections with six nested DIV3 month sections.

Cataloging applies to the medieval angling work represented on Satchell pp. 1–37. It excludes the encoded 1883 title page as an edition-level part and excludes all unencoded carrier matter. The four source-segment content parts record Satchell's manuscript/print construction and are not claimed as intrinsic medieval literary divisions.

The work is cataloged as anonymous. Satchell's title says 'attributed to Dame Juliana Barnes,' and later catalogs often use Berners as a creator heading, but scholarship distinguishes the named hunting explicit from the fishing treatise added to the 1496 printing and treats the extended attribution as disputed.

assertion.notes: Capitalization is normalized for display while retaining the historically attested wording.

titles.supplied_by: Normalized from the 1496 incipit-title and Satchell's source-edition title

work.regionDisplay: England; exact place of composition unknown
work.summary: An anonymous Middle English prose treatise presenting angling as a healthful and morally regulated recreation. After comparing fishing favorably with hunting, hawking, and fowling, it teaches the making and coloring of rods and lines, hooks, floats, methods, suitable places, times and weather, impediments, fish-specific and seasonal baits, and the care of bait. Its final order prohibits trespass, theft, and excessive taking, urges stewardship of fish stocks, and frames solitary angling as conducive to bodily health, prayer, and avoidance of idleness. Two brief Latin maxims—four lines total—are quoted and explained in Middle English. The CME text represents Satchell's 1883 composite edition, which joins two surviving manuscript runs to two passages supplied from the 1496 printed Treatyse.
